invariant_definition: Difference, distinction. A core Vedāntic term whose status is exactly the locus of school-disagreement: Advaita holds *bheda* to be ultimately illusory (the *bhedābhāva* — non-existence of difference — is the highest truth); Dvaita holds *bheda* to be eternally real and irreducible (the *pañca-bheda* doctrine); Bhedābheda holds *bheda* and *abheda* to be equally and simultaneously real. Madhva's metaphysics is sometimes summarized as *atyanta-bheda-vāda* — the doctrine of absolute difference.

per_school.definition: *Bheda* between *brahman* and *jīva* and between *jīva* and *jīva* is *vyāvahārika* only; in *pāramārthika* there is no *bheda*. The Upaniṣadic *neha nānāsti kiñcana* (Bṛhad. 4.4.19) is read as the absolute denial of difference.

The *pañca-bheda* — five eternal distinctions (Brahman–jīva, Brahman–matter, jīva–jīva, jīva–matter, matter–matter) — is foundational. *Bheda* is *svarūpa* (intrinsic), *anādi* (beginningless), *ananta* (endless), and *atyanta* (absolute).
*Bheda* and *abheda* are equally real, simultaneously obtaining. The opposition between them is not contradiction but the dialectical structure of reality itself.

*Bheda* and *abheda* simultaneously obtain *acintya* — beyond logical resolution; the relation is real but its logical structure exceeds reason. *Sākṣād-vacana* (direct scriptural utterance) is the only *pramāṇa* for it.

translator_note: 'Difference' is the standard gloss. Critical: in technical Vedānta *bheda* is not weakly 'difference between' but ontological 'real distinction' — the question is whether reality at its base contains distinctions.
